description: console.info("msg", {obj}) renders as "msg {}" in the Next 16 dev logger — the structured object is silently dropped. This bit us where a webhook paid-order log was the only durable trace of a sale. Separately, Next 16 refuses to start a second dev server on another port.
resolution: Serialise the object into the message string with JSON.stringify rather than passing it as a second argument. For dev servers, reuse the already-running one.

description: Both mcp__playwright and mcp__chrome-devtools refused to launch ('Browser is already in use ... use --isolated') because a parallel Claude session held the shared browser profile. npm i playwright then failed too: no cached chromium_headless_shell binary.
resolution: Install playwright with --no-save into the scratchpad and launch via chromium.launch({ channel: 'chrome' }) to drive the system Chrome instead of downloading a browser. This verified the sticky bar end-to-end (off-screen+inert at top, visible when scrolled, display:none on desktop, absent on /checkout) without touching the locked MCP profiles.

description: Edits to Next.js components had zero effect in the browser — hard reloads, clean tsc, no console errors. Cause: port 3000 was held by a 'next-server' process (next start), serving a prebuilt .next bundle with no HMR and no recompilation. Spent several iterations 'tuning' code the browser never loaded. Diagnose by checking 'ps aux | grep next': 'next-server' = production (next start); 'next dev' shows Turbopack banner on startup.
resolution: Before visually iterating on a Next.js UI, confirm the dev server is actually dev: lsof -nP -iTCP:3000 -sTCP:LISTEN, then ps the PID. If it's next-server, kill it and run 'npm run dev'. Fastest proof the bundle is live: make an absurd probe change (e.g. camera distance 820 -> 2400); if the render doesn't change, the server is stale — stop tuning and fix the server.
